patient_information.name: Michael Andrew Patterson
patient_information.address: 4827 S Harvard Ave, Apt 3B, Tulsa, OK 74135
patient_information.allergies: Allopurinol – developed diffuse pruritic maculopapular rash and facial swelling in 2020 (documented drug allergy, avoided since). Colchicine – significant gastrointestinal intolerance (severe diarrhea and abdominal cramping at standard dosing). No known allergies to contrast dyes, latex, or foods. Tolerates NSAIDs (ibuprofen, naproxen) without issue.

krystexxa_order.krystexxa_order:
Initiate KRYSTEXXA (pegloticase) 8 mg IV every 2 weeks for uncontrolled chronic gout with tophaceous deposits. Administer as intravenous infusion over at least 120 minutes via peripheral IV line. Pre-medicate with:
- Fexofenadine 180 mg PO 60 minutes prior to each infusion
- Acetaminophen 650 mg PO 60 minutes prior to each infusion
- Methylprednisolone 40 mg IV 30 minutes prior to each infusion (or equivalent corticosteroid per infusion center protocol)

Monitor vital signs (BP, HR, RR, O2 saturation, and temperature) prior to infusion, every 30 minutes during infusion, and for at least 60 minutes post-infusion to watch for infusion or anaphylactic reactions. Have epinephrine, IV antihistamines, and corticosteroids immediately available per anaphylaxis protocol.

Hold KRYSTEXXA infusion if pre-infusion serum uric acid is > 6 mg/dL on two consecutive readings, and notify ordering physician prior to resuming therapy. Avoid concurrent use of oral urate-lowering therapies (e.g., allopurinol, febuxostat, probenecid) while patient is receiving KRYSTEXXA, as this may mask loss of efficacy and increase risk of infusion reactions.

Expected duration of therapy: reassess clinical response and safety after 6 months (approximately 13 infusions). Continue or adjust regimen per clinical response and payer requirements. Infusion center to contact office for any severe infusion reactions, urticaria, bronchospasm, hypotension, or other significant adverse events.
krystexxa_order.icd_10: M1A.0711 – Chronic gout due to renal impairment, right ankle and foot, with tophus (tophi)

standing_lab_orders.frequency: Draw CBC and CMP every 8 weeks while on KRYSTEXXA therapy, beginning at baseline (prior to first infusion), then at weeks 8, 16, 24, and as clinically indicated for any adverse symptoms (e.g., new fatigue, easy bruising, or changes in blood pressure). Fax results to (918) 555-7191 and flag critical values urgently to the ordering physician.

additional_comments.notes_additional_comments:
Patient is a 60-year-old male with long-standing, poorly controlled chronic tophaceous gout despite multiple prior urate-lowering therapies. He has visible tophi over the right first metatarsophalangeal joint, right Achilles tendon, and bilateral elbows, with recurrent flares (6–8 per year) significantly limiting his mobility and work as an HVAC technician. He has stage 3a chronic kidney disease (baseline creatinine around 1.5–1.7 mg/dL), which has limited our ability to escalate allopurinol and NSAID dosing in the past.

He previously trialed allopurinol up to 300 mg daily but developed a generalized pruritic rash and mild facial swelling, leading to discontinuation and documentation as a drug allergy. Febuxostat was trialed for approximately 5 months with only modest reduction in serum uric acid (persistently > 8 mg/dL) and ongoing flares. Colchicine is poorly tolerated due to severe diarrhea; he only uses it very sparingly for flares. He has been using intermittent low-dose prednisone tapers for acute attacks, but we are trying to minimize cumulative steroid exposure due to prediabetes and elevated blood pressure.

Baseline serum uric acid values over the last 12 months have consistently been above 9 mg/dL, with the most recent documented level at 9.8 mg/dL (drawn 2024-11-21). Radiographs demonstrate erosive changes in the right first MTP joint and soft tissue tophus. G6PD testing completed on 2024-10-30 was within normal limits and is attached with this referral.

Patient has been counseled extensively on the risks and benefits of KRYSTEXXA, including the potential for infusion reactions, anaphylaxis, and the need for regular infusions and monitoring. He understands that oral urate-lowering agents should be discontinued during therapy and has already stopped febuxostat as of 2024-12-01. He is motivated to proceed given the impact of gout on his ability to work and perform daily activities (he reports having to miss multiple days of work in the past year due to severe ankle and foot flares).

Please schedule infusions on Thursday or Friday mornings if possible, as the patient typically arranges his HVAC service calls earlier in the week and can more easily take time off at the end of the week. He has reliable transportation and prefers phone calls over patient portals; however, he does routinely check his email and is comfortable receiving appointment reminders electronically. He lives approximately 15 minutes from the Tulsa infusion center. No history of prior infusion reactions to other medications. Blood pressure at last office visit was 132/78 mmHg; he is on lisinopril 10 mg daily for hypertension.

If there are any questions regarding the order, pre-medication regimen, or lab schedule, please contact our infusion coordinator, Rebecca Owens, RN, at the office number listed above. For any severe adverse reactions or unexpected findings on labs (e.g., significant drop in hemoglobin, platelets, or worsening renal function), please page Dr. Chen through the clinic answering service for same-day consultation.
